steps.title: Neurodevelopmental disorders
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: Which of the following is considered the foundation of treatment for ADHD?
steps.rationale: Psychoeducation is identified as the foundation of treatment because ADHD is a chronic condition. It helps families understand and manage the condition.

textChoices.text: Methylphenidate therapy
Psychoeducation
Cardiac assessment
Neuroimaging

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: Before starting medication for ADHD, what baseline evaluation is essential?
steps.rationale: A full clinical assessment should be conducted before initiating ADHD medication. This includes reviewing mental health, social circumstances, and physical health.

textChoices.text: Chest X-ray
Thyroid function test
Full clinical assessment including psychosocial review
Spirometry

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: When should a cardiology opinion be sought before starting ADHD medication?
steps.rationale: Cardiology referral is warranted if there’s a history of congenital heart disease, cardiac symptoms, or sudden death in a first-degree relative.

textChoices.text: For all patients under 10 years old
History of congenital heart disease or sudden death in a first-degree relative
Elevated cholesterol
If the child has asthma

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: Which medication is the recommended first-line pharmacological treatment for children aged 5 years and older with ADHD?
steps.rationale: Methylphenidate (short or long-acting) is recommended as the first-line pharmacologic treatment for ADHD in children over 5 years.

textChoices.text: Risperidone
Guanfacine
Diazepam
Methylphenidate

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding medication for ADHD at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Only professionals with adequate training in ADHD should initiate medication due to the need for careful assessment and monitoring.

textChoices.text: Any healthcare worker may prescribe it
It should be initiated by a professional with training in ADHD
It must be started before behavioural therapy
Medication should be the only form of treatment

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: What should be assessed before initiating stimulant therapy in ADHD?
steps.rationale: Stimulants can affect cardiovascular parameters, so baseline pulse and blood pressure must be assessed.

textChoices.text: Allergy testing
Blood pressure and heart rate
Vitamin D levels
Renal ultrasound

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: Why should height and weight be recorded before and during stimulant use?
steps.rationale: Stimulants such as methylphenidate are known to possibly affect growth, so monitoring height and weight is necessary.

textChoices.text: To assess for thyroid dysfunction
Stimulants may suppress growth
For body mass index only
To determine correct medication dose

steps.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD)
steps.text: When may risperidone be considered for a child with ADHD?
steps.rationale: Risperidone should only be used when behavioural difficulties persist and psychological/behavioural interventions have failed.

textChoices.text: Only if there is comorbid behavioural difficulty and psychological interventions have failed
In children under 5
As a first-line treatment
Alongside methylphenidate from the start

steps.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder
steps.text: What is a key responsibility of tertiary-level services in the care of children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, tertiary-level services are responsible for assessing, diagnosing, and managing children with ASD.

textChoices.text: Provide school-based behavioral observations only
Refer the patient to secondary care for diagnosis
Assess, diagnose, and manage the child
Delay diagnosis until adolescence

steps.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder
steps.text: In addition to diagnosing ASD, what else is expected from tertiary-level services according to the guideline?
steps.rationale: The guideline clearly states that tertiary services must also support other care levels in the ongoing management of patients with ASD.

textChoices.text: Support lower-level facilities in the ongoing care of children with ASD
Handle only medication-related concerns
Monitor educational progress exclusively
Discharge patients after diagnosis

steps.title: Intellectual Disability (ID)
steps.text: What is a core responsibility of tertiary-level services in the management of children with intellectual disability (ID)?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that tertiary-level services are responsible for assessment, diagnosis, management, and supporting lower-level facilities in the ongoing care of children with ID.

textChoices.text: Provide home-based educational instruction
Refer directly to primary care for treatment
Assess, diagnose, and manage the child, and support other care levels
Limit involvement to behavioral therapy only

steps.title: Specific Learning Disability/Disorders
steps.text: Which of the following best describes the role of tertiary-level facilities in the management of specific learning disabilities?
steps.rationale: The guideline clearly states that tertiary-level services are critical in making the diagnosis of specific learning disabilities, with collaboration from other care levels.

textChoices.text: They provide routine classroom teaching
They only offer speech therapy services
They refer all suspected cases to secondary care
Tertiary-level facilities are critical in making the diagnosis

steps.title: Specific Learning Disability/Disorders
steps.text: In addition to diagnosing specific learning disorders, what is a key responsibility of tertiary-level services?
steps.rationale: Tertiary facilities must coordinate with providers at other care levels to confirm and support the diagnosis through shared information and collaboration.

textChoices.text: Focus only on IQ testing
Refer all cases to school authorities
Conduct interventions independently
Liaise with primary and secondary level providers to substantiate the diagnosis

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: What is the first-line approach for treating emotional distress in children at secondary or tertiary care levels?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes that skills-based therapy (e.g., cognitive restructuring, relaxation, psychoeducation) is the mainstay of treatment, with medication reserved for severe cases.

textChoices.text: Medication combined with inpatient admission
Weekly psychiatric evaluations only
Skills-based therapy programs
Emergency use of antidepressants

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: Which of the following should be included as part of a structured skills-based therapy plan?
steps.rationale: Skills-based programs listed in the guideline include exposure therapy, parent training, relaxation, cognitive restructuring, and more — all non-pharmacologic approaches.

textChoices.text: Electroconvulsive therapy and medication
CT brain scan and diet control
Antipsychotic therapy and school exclusion
Exposure therapy, parent training, and social skills training

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: What is the recommended approach to using medication for children with emotional distress, according to the guideline?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises that drugs should be used only if symptoms are very severe, and must be initiated carefully, preferably with input from a clinician experienced in pediatric psychopharmacology.

textChoices.text: Medication should be used as the first-line treatment in all cases
Drugs should be prescribed immediately if the child appears withdrawn
Medication should be given at the parent’s request
Medication should only be used as a last resort when symptoms are very severe

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: How often should children receiving skills-based therapy be reviewed during the early phase of treatment?
steps.rationale: Patients should be reviewed every 2–4 weeks, especially during early treatment, to monitor progress and adjust therapy as needed.

textChoices.text: Once a year
Every 2–4 weeks
Only when medication is started
After 6 months of treatment

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: When should drug treatment be considered in the management of emotional distress in children?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, medication is a last option, used only if symptoms are severe or the child does not respond to skills-based therapy.

textChoices.text: In all cases as first-line care
After 3 therapy sessions
If symptoms are severe or there is no response to therapy
Based on parental request alone

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: What critical safety assessment must be performed for all children with emotional distress?
steps.rationale: Clinicians must always assess for suicide risk. Any suicidal thoughts or behaviors must lead to urgent referral for specialist intervention.

textChoices.text: Vision and hearing screening
Suicide risk assessment with urgent referral if necessary
Nutritional scoring
Medication trial before diagnosis

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: What is the recommended duration for continuing medication in children with emotional distress to prevent relapse?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises continuing medication for up to 12 months, followed by slow weaning and close monitoring for relapse.

textChoices.text: Stop as soon as symptoms improve
Continue for life
Up to 12 months with gradual weaning
Until the child starts school

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: What is the correct approach to monitoring children on SSRIs for emotional distress, according to the guideline?
steps.rationale: The guideline stresses that all children on medication, especially SSRIs, must be closely monitored for worsening symptoms, including suicidal ideation or behavior, and that caregivers should be informed and involved. Referral to mental health professionals should be made as needed.

textChoices.text: Monitor for physical side effects only
No follow-up is needed if symptoms improve initially
Monitor closely for worsening symptoms and advise caregivers to do the same
Refer only if sleep disturbances occur

steps.title: Stress, Anxiety and other emotional distress
steps.text: According to the guideline, what should be done if a child on SSRIs shows signs of worsening symptoms or suicidal ideation?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes that if any child on treatment—especially SSRIs—shows worsening behavior or suicidal thoughts, they must be referred promptly to a psychiatry unit or mental health professional for appropriate intervention.

textChoices.text: Stop the medication and monitor at home
Increase the dose to stabilize the mood
Wait for the next scheduled review before taking action
Refer the child to a psychiatry unit or mental health practitioner for assessment and treatment

steps.title: Depression
steps.text: What is a key responsibility of tertiary-level facilities in the management of depression?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that tertiary-level services are responsible for handling complex or complicated cases of depression that require advanced expertise.

textChoices.text: Provide basic counseling only
Refer all patients back to primary care
Assess, diagnose, and manage complicated cases
Avoid pharmacological treatment options

steps.title: Depression
steps.text: What is the recommended course of action for a child presenting with severe depression and significant suicide risk?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises that children with high-risk symptoms such as suicidal ideation should be assessed promptly for inpatient psychiatric care, ensuring safety and intensive support.

textChoices.text: Evaluate for possible inpatient admission to a psychiatric unit
Initiate outpatient therapy and review after one month
Refer the case to school-based counseling services
Delay admission unless self-harm has already occurred

steps.title: Suicide / self-harm
steps.text: What is a responsibility of secondary or tertiary-level facilities in managing patients with PTSD or self-harm risk?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, secondary and tertiary facilities should assess and manage patients with PTSD or self-harm if equipped to do so.

textChoices.text: Refer all cases to primary level for initial diagnosis
Assess and manage patients if the services are available at that level
Only monitor for physical injuries
Focus solely on group therapy sessions

steps.title: Suicide / self-harm
steps.text: When can a primary-level facility take over the care of a patient with PTSD or history of self-harm?
steps.rationale: The guideline notes that ongoing care for stable patients may be continued at the primary level, provided the right skills and services are in place.

textChoices.text: Immediately after the first episode resolves
Only after psychiatric admission
Once medication is started at the tertiary level
When the patient is stable and the necessary skills and services are available

steps.title: Suicide / self-harm
steps.text: A 14-year-old on SSRIs for depression begins showing increased irritability and vague references to hopelessness. What is the most appropriate next step according to the guideline?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that any sign of worsening behavior or suicidal ideation in a child on treatment—especially SSRIs—warrants urgent referral. Caregivers must also be actively involved in monitoring at home.

textChoices.text: Reduce the SSRI dose and continue monitoring during routine follow-up
Wait for a clear verbal expression of suicidal intent before acting
Advise school staff to watch for further behavior changes
Urgently refer the child for specialist assessment and advise caregivers to monitor closely

steps.title: Psychosis
steps.text: What is a key responsibility of tertiary-level care in managing a child with psychosis?
steps.rationale: The guideline states that tertiary-level facilities are responsible for assessing and starting acute management of psychosis in children.

textChoices.text: Refer immediately to secondary care for all suspected cases
Limit management to supportive therapy only
Assess and initiate acute management
Delay treatment until long-term causes are identified

steps.title: Psychosis
steps.text: What should be done after stabilizing a child with psychosis at the tertiary level, according to the guideline?
steps.rationale: Once the child is stabilized and treatment is underway, the guideline advises to rule out organic causes, and ongoing care may be shifted to secondary-level facilities if services are available.

textChoices.text: Rule out organic causes and consider continuing care at secondary level if appropriate
Discharge the child to home care with no follow-up
Refer directly to school counseling services
Start indefinite inpatient care regardless of progress
